FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study baseline data, 10,712 children ages 9-10 years from 21 U.S. sites

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time is component of outcome (movement behavior composite), not the exposure. Exposures are parental acceptance, monitoring, and family conflict. Screen time measurement used only for outcome categorization.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multilevel logistic regression examining association between family typologies and meeting movement behavior recommendations; multiple tests reported

FT_IC5.evidence: Cross-sectional empirical analysis using latent profile analysis and logistic regression on ABCD baseline data

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, 2021

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; predictors are family environment characteristics (parental acceptance, monitoring, conflict); outcome is movement behavior (non-SM), not screen media. FT_EC7 exclusion criterion not met.
notes: Edge case E2 applies: screen time is part of 24-h movement composite (physical activity, screen time, sleep). Only aggregate composite outcome reported, not screen-time-specific coefficient. This disqualifies FT_IC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study baseline data, 9,718 children ages 9-11 years from 21 U.S. sites

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen media activity measured as specific variable: weekday/weekend breakdown including social network sites, text/instant messages, TV shows/movies, R-rated movies, videos (YouTube), video games

FT_IC3.evidence: Machine learning framework identifies associations between screen media activity and fluid cognitive functioning; multiple statistical tests conducted via ML algorithm

FT_IC4.evidence: Variable importance (VI) metrics reported for screen media activity; Table 2 shows statistical values including VI estimates for weekday/weekend screen media activity

FT_IC5.evidence: Empirical ML analysis of ABCD baseline data identifying predictors of cognitive functioning

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Child Development, September/October 2021

FT_EC7_reason: Screen media activity is predictor (IV) of fluid cognitive functioning (non-SM outcome), not the dependent variable. FT_EC7 applies only when SM is DV with demographic-only predictors in longitudinal design.
notes: Edge case E4: ML analysis with variable importance metrics reported, which qualifies as extractable coefficient for FT_IC4. Not SHAP/Gini alone.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study baseline and one-year follow-up data, 11,025 children ages 9-10 years at baseline from 21 U.S. sites

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time modalities measured: social networking (aOR 1.62), texting (aOR 1.40), watching/streaming TV shows/movies (aOR 1.39), videos (YouTube), video games, video chat. Each modality separately assessed.

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic regression analyses testing associations between baseline screen time and binge-eating disorder at one-year follow-up; multiple screen time types tested independently

FT_IC4.evidence: ORs with 95% CIs reported: total screen time aOR 1.11 (1.05-1.18); social networking aOR 1.62 (1.18-2.22); texting aOR 1.40 (1.08-1.82); TV aOR 1.39 (1.14-1.69)

FT_IC5.evidence: Prospective cohort empirical analysis with logistic regression, baseline exposure measured and one-year follow-up outcome assessed

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in International Journal of Eating Disorders, 2021

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is independent variable (IV) predicting binge-eating disorder outcome. Paper includes screen media → non-SM outcome analysis. FT_EC7 does not apply.
notes: Prospective design with clear smartphone/social media exposure (social networking, texting) and clinical outcome (binge-eating disorder based on KSADS-5 DSM-5 criteria). All inclusion criteria met.
